bio.title: Rev.
bio.bio_blurb: Osage and Cherokee missionary; San Antonio pioneer

narrative_blocks.header: He buried a wife and child mid-journey
narrative_blocks.body: In 1821 Samuel Newton, his wife Betsey, and their children set out from New York with the United Foreign Missionary Society to establish Harmony Mission among the Osage. On the journey Betsey died near Shawneetown, Illinois, days after their newborn died near Louisville — a double loss before the mission was even reached.

narrative_blocks.header: He married two missionary widows in turn
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed on the trail, Samuel married Mary Hunt McCarty at Harmony Mission in 1822, the widow of fellow missionary John Seeley. After Mary died in 1835 he married a third missionary widow, Sophia Palmer Joslyn, whose first husband had also died in the field.

narrative_blocks.header: The first Protestant to settle San Antonio
narrative_blocks.body: After missions to the Osage and Cherokee, Samuel brought his family to San Antonio in 1848, when the town had scarcely a shingled roof among its adobe houses. Family record holds he was the first Protestant to settle there permanently; he opened a private school and helped found the city’s First Presbyterian Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Six generations of Samuels before him
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Samuel Newton was the sixth Samuel Newton in unbroken succession — Samuel son of Samuel son of Samuel, back six generations to the immigrant minister Roger Newton. He carried that Connecticut lineage from a Woodbridge parsonage to the Texas frontier.

cross_connections.display_label: served the same Cherokee mission field in Indian Territory

narrative_blocks.header: At twenty-one she went to the Osage
narrative_blocks.body: Sophia Palmer, youngest of four sisters whose father died serving in the War of 1812, became a missionary at twenty-one — first to the Osage, then the Cherokee in Indian Territory. She married two missionaries in turn, burying the first at Dwight Mission in 1833.

narrative_blocks.header: She helped found a frontier church
narrative_blocks.body: After reaching San Antonio in 1848, Sophia taught school — many old San Antonians recalled being her pupils — and was a founder of the city’s First Presbyterian Church, organized in 1851. She outlived Samuel by thirty-four years, dying in 1899.
